planting.note: Plant dormant pseudobulbs 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost; established clumps can be divided in early spring or fall. In zone 7–9 Chinese ground orchid is reliably perennial, dying back to the ground in winter and re-emerging each spring to bloom in April–May before the foliage fully expands.

directions: Plant pseudobulbs 2–3 inches deep in rich, well-drained, humus-rich soil in part shade to full shade; Chinese ground orchid is one of the very few true hardy orchids suitable for temperate garden conditions, producing charming magenta-pink to white flowers on upright spikes in April–May before the pleated, strap-like foliage reaches full height. It is one of the most effective shade garden plants for adding color in spring when few other shade perennials are in bloom. Clumps expand slowly over years — never dig out the entire clump; instead take divisions from the perimeter while leaving the main clump undisturbed. Provide a light mulch in zone 5–6 winters; in zone 7–9 plants are generally fully hardy without protection. Avoid waterlogged soils, which cause pseudobulb rot.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Bletilla striata Plant Finder
University of Georgia Extension — Hardy Orchids for Southern Gardens
American Orchid Society — Bletilla (Hardy Orchid) Cultivation

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March. Leutschauer Paprika is a slow-to-ripen variety — allow the full season for fruits to turn deep red; in zone 9 the long warm season suits this variety particularly well and allows full color development without rushing.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Leutschauer Paprika is a Slovak heirloom paprika pepper that produces elongated, pendant, 5–6 inch fruits with exceptionally thin walls and high sugar content — characteristics that make it one of the premier varieties for drying and grinding into fresh paprika. The bright red, fully ripe fruits are dried whole in strings (paprika ristras) or sliced and dehydrated before grinding. The flavor is sweet, slightly fruity, and aromatic — quite different from commercially ground paprika that has lost volatile compounds in processing. Plants are productive on fertile soil with consistent moisture; fruit set can stall during the hottest weeks in zone 9 but resumes in September. This is primarily a drying variety, though the fresh ripe fruits are also edible.
sources: Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Paprika Types
University of New Mexico — Southwest Pepper Drying Traditions

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March for spring production. Talladega is a heat-set determinate hybrid with excellent blemish resistance in humid conditions — it is among the most reliable heavy-cropping varieties for the hot, humid Gulf Coast and Deep South.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil with a cage or stake; Talladega is a determinate, heat-tolerant hybrid tomato that produces large harvests of smooth, firm, 8–10 ounce red globe tomatoes with excellent shelf life and crack resistance. It was bred specifically for commercial and home production in the hot, humid South, where disease pressure from early blight, grey leaf spot, and bacterial spot is severe — Talladega carries broad disease resistance that keeps foliage healthy through the summer months when other varieties defoliate. The concentrated fruiting habit of this determinate variety makes it easy to time a large harvest for canning or preserving. Fall plantings in late July–early August often produce the heaviest yields as the fruits ripen into the cooler October–November weather.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Varieties for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tomato Variety Trials
North Carolina State Extension — Tomato Variety Recommendations

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March after nights stay above 55°F. Lorz Italian is productive through the Gulf Coast summer and performs best when harvested green; if left to ripen red, pick before fruits become overripe and mushy in summer humidity.

directions: Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Lorz Italian is a Washington State heirloom sweet frying pepper, a classic Italian-style variety with thin-walled, pendent fruits that are 8–10 inches long — among the longest sweet peppers available. The mild, sweet flesh is nearly seedless and ideal for the classic Italian-American preparation of frying in olive oil with garlic and salt (peperonata), as well as roasting, stuffing, and fresh eating. The thin walls roast and collapse beautifully without the wateriness of thicker-walled sweet peppers. Plants produce prolifically once established, with multiple fruits per branch; the long, elegant fruits are also ornamental hanging from the plant. Harvest green for crispest texture or allow to ripen red for sweetest flavor.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Lorz Italian Pepper
Washington State University Extension — Heritage Vegetable Varieties
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Sweet Frying Pepper Types

planting.note: Start indoors 8 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March. Charleston Hot is a Southern cayenne-type that was bred for the humid, hot conditions of the Carolinas and Gulf Coast and has strong resistance to tobacco etch virus and tobacco mosaic virus — significant disease pressure in zone 8–9 pepper production.

directions: Transplant into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Charleston Hot is a Clemson University-developed cayenne-type hot pepper (70,000–100,000 SHU) bred specifically for production in the hot, humid South. An All-America Selections winner, it produces abundantly on compact, upright plants and carries strong disease resistance to tobacco etch virus and tobacco mosaic virus, which is valuable in zone 8–9 where solanaceous virus pressure is high. The slim, tapered, 3–4 inch fruits ripen from yellow-green through yellow-orange to red; they are used fresh, dried, or ground into hot sauce and cayenne powder. The heavy yield of small-fruited plants makes this variety excellent for hot sauce production — a single mature plant can yield hundreds of pods per season. Dry fully ripe red fruits in a dehydrator or oven for ground cayenne.
sources: Clemson University Cooperative Extension — Charleston Hot Pepper
All-America Selections — Charleston Hot Award Record
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hot Pepper Production
